agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A courier-tracking feature is proposed to show customers their courier's real-time location during delivery. Two internal stakeholder teams have issued directly conflicting, non-negotiable requirements about whether courier GPS location should be visible to customers.","bounded_context":"Delivery Tracking / Shipment Visibility","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Courier","definition":"A driver or delivery person who physically transports a package from origin to the customer's delivery address."},{"term":"Real-time location","definition":"The GPS coordinates of a courier, updated continuously or at short intervals during an active delivery."},{"term":"Delivery","definition":"The active fulfillment leg where a courier is en route to the customer with their package."},{"term":"Tracking","definition":"The customer-facing capability to observe the progress of their delivery."},{"term":"Transparency","definition":"Customer Success's principle that customers should have full visibility into delivery status and courier position."},{"term":"Courier retention","definition":"Operations' metric measuring the ability to keep drivers working on the platform, directly threatened by surveillance concerns."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Customers must have visibility into the progress of their active delivery.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Without any delivery visibility, the platform fails its core value proposition to customers. Some form of tracking is a table-stakes expectation in modern logistics.","testable_assertion":"Given an active delivery, when a customer views the tracking page, then they see meaningful progress information about their delivery."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Courier location data must be collected during active deliveries for operational purposes (routing, ETA calculation, dispatch).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"GPS telemetry is essential for platform operations regardless of whether it is exposed to customers. Without it, ETAs, routing, and dispatch cannot function.","testable_assertion":"Given a courier on an active delivery, when the courier's device reports GPS coordinates, then the system persists the location for operational use."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Courier privacy preferences must be respected to maintain driver retention and platform supply health.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The specific privacy mechanism is a policy decision, but the principle that courier welfare affects platform viability is a hard business constraint. The exact implementation is negotiable.","testable_assertion":"Given a courier with privacy concerns, when the platform's tracking policy is applied, then the courier's exact real-time coordinates are not directly exposed to end customers without a privacy-preserving abstraction."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"The tracking experience must provide customers with actionable delivery information (ETA, delivery status, proximity).","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The specific data shown is a product/policy decision. Customers need useful information, but exact GPS coordinates are not the only way to deliver it.","testable_assertion":"Given an active delivery, when a customer checks tracking, then they see an estimated time of arrival and a delivery status indicator."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A customer has an active delivery in progress","when":"they open the tracking page","then":"they see the current delivery status (e.g., picked up, en route, nearby, delivered) and an estimated time of arrival"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A courier is actively delivering a package","when":"the system collects GPS telemetry from the courier's device","then":"the raw GPS coordinates are stored for internal operational use only and are not directly transmitted to the customer-facing tracking interface"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A customer is viewing the tracking page for an active delivery","when":"the courier is en route","then":"the customer sees a progress indicator (e.g., abstracted map zone, distance range, or ETA countdown) rather than exact latitude/longitude coordinates"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A delivery transitions to a terminal state (delivered or failed)","when":"the tracking page is viewed","then":"no courier location data of any kind is displayed"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Courier GPS signal is lost during delivery","expected_behavior":"The tracking page shows last known status and ETA with a 'last updated' timestamp. No stale exact coordinates are shown."},{"scenario":"Customer contacts support asking for exact courier location","expected_behavior":"Support agents may have access to precise location for operational troubleshooting, but the customer-facing response should use abstracted terms (e.g., '5 minutes away', 'in your neighborhood')."},{"scenario":"Courier is stationary for an extended period mid-delivery","expected_behavior":"System should not reveal this to the customer as an exact pinned location. ETA updates or a status like 'en route' should be shown."},{"scenario":"Multiple deliveries by the same courier","expected_behavior":"Customers only see tracking for their own delivery. No cross-customer location leakage."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Losing couriers (supply side) has direct revenue impact — fewer drivers means longer delivery times, lower capacity, and higher acquisition costs for replacement drivers. Customer churn from poor tracking is also a revenue risk but is addressable with abstracted tracking. The cost of over-sharing location is harder to recover from than under-sharing.","operational":"Courier attrition increases recruitment and training costs. A privacy-preserving tracking approach requires engineering investment in abstraction layers (geo-fencing, ETA engines) but avoids the operational crisis of driver shortages.","user":"Customers want confidence their delivery is coming — this can be satisfied with ETA, status updates, and proximity indicators without exact GPS. Couriers want to feel like professionals, not surveilled gig workers.","risk":"Showing exact GPS coordinates introduces personal safety risks for couriers (e.g., customers intercepting them en route) and potential regulatory exposure under GDPR/CCPA for sharing employee/contractor location data with third parties. NOT showing any tracking risks customer satisfaction.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Will receive delivery progress information through abstracted indicators rather than raw GPS. Core need (knowing when delivery arrives) is still met."},{"group":"Couriers/Drivers","impact":"Privacy is preserved, reducing surveillance anxiety and improving retention. Their exact movements are not broadcast to customers."},{"group":"Customer Success team","impact":"Must reframe 'transparency' as actionable delivery visibility rather than raw coordinate sharing. Their goal of differentiation can be met with superior ETA accuracy and proactive notifications."},{"group":"Operations team","impact":"Driver retention concern is addressed. They retain full internal access to GPS data for dispatch and routing."},{"group":"Legal/Compliance","impact":"Sharing precise real-time worker location with third parties has GDPR/CCPA implications that must be evaluated. Abstracted tracking reduces this exposure."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"Two explicit, non-negotiable stakeholder requirements directly contradict each other. Neither team has been overruled or has acknowledged the other's constraint. Until executive arbitration resolves which business priority (customer transparency vs. courier retention) takes precedence, no implementation can satisfy both stated requirements simultaneously. Confidence is low because the spec is fundamentally unresolvable without a decision-maker above both teams.","high_confidence_areas":["GPS telemetry must be collected for internal operations regardless of customer visibility","Some form of delivery tracking is a table-stakes requirement for customers","Exact GPS coordinate sharing creates courier privacy and safety risks","An abstracted tracking approach (ETA, status, proximity) is a viable compromise that exists in the industry"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether executive leadership will side with Customer Success, Operations, or a middle-ground approach","Regulatory requirements around sharing contractor/employee location data with customers in the operating jurisdictions","Whether couriers would accept any form of abstracted location sharing or reject all tracking visibility"],"assumptions":["A privacy-preserving abstraction (ETA, status, geo-zone) would satisfy the core customer need for delivery visibility","Courier retention is a genuine operational crisis, not a negotiating position","Customer Success's 'transparency' goal can be reframed around delivery confidence rather than raw data exposure"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"Customer Success requires showing exact courier GPS coordinates to customers as a non-negotiable transparency feature. Operations requires never showing courier location to customers as a non-negotiable retention measure. These are mutually exclusive requirements from peer-level teams with no stated resolution authority.","affected_stakeholders":["Customer Success","Operations","Couriers","Customers","Executive Leadership"],"options":["A: Side with Customer Success — show exact GPS. Risk: accelerated courier attrition, potential regulatory exposure, safety concerns.","B: Side with Operations — show no location data at all. Risk: customer dissatisfaction, loss of competitive differentiation.","C: Compromise with abstracted tracking — show ETA, delivery status, and proximity zones instead of exact coordinates. Satisfies the customer need for visibility while preserving courier privacy. Industry standard approach (Uber, DoorDash use abstracted representations)."],"recommendation":"Option C. The logistics industry has converged on abstracted tracking (smoothed map animations, ETA countdowns, status milestones) precisely because it resolves this exact tension. Exact GPS coordinates are neither necessary for customer confidence nor safe for courier welfare. This requires executive arbitration to align both teams, but the domain-correct answer is clear: show delivery progress, not driver surveillance."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Showing exact courier GPS coordinates to customers","business_rule_broken":"BR-3 — Courier privacy must be respected to maintain supply-side health. Exact coordinate sharing constitutes surveillance and creates safety, retention, and regulatory risks."},{"violation":"Showing zero delivery tracking information to customers","business_rule_broken":"BR-1 — Customers must have visibility into delivery progress. Complete absence of tracking fails the platform's core value proposition."}]}
agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A courier-tracking feature is proposed to show customers their courier's real-time location during delivery. Two internal stakeholder teams have issued directly conflicting, non-negotiable requirements about whether courier GPS location should be visible to customers.","bounded_context":"Delivery Tracking / Shipment Visibility","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Courier","definition":"A driver or delivery person who physically transports a package from origin

content.thinking: Clear contradiction between two stakeholder teams. Both explicit, both non-negotiable, directly conflicting.
